name: Cova des Pas de Vallgornera
footwear: Cut and perforated rubber boots are ideal
clothing: Neoprene essential, plus dry clothes (shorts and t-shirt)
access: Own vehicle. Location coordinates cannot be disclosed.
equipment: Neoprene, fins, electric light (carbide not permitted). Waterproof container for dry clothes and food, canyoning backpack.
description: One of the world's longest caves with over 55km of mapped passages as of 2008, ranking 24th globally. Discovered in 1968 during construction at Hotel Es Pas, it features extraordinary formations including moonmilk, stunning flooded galleries, and the enormous Sala que no Té Nom. Access is strictly restricted under EU Red Natura 2000 (LIC ES5310049) and requires special permission.
full_description:
Due mainly to the dimensions of this cave — more than 55 km of development as of June 2008, the twenty-fourth longest in the world at that time — I cannot provide a classic description of it, since that would require first knowing all the explored areas of the cave, which is not the case. So what I will try to do is structure the information by giving a brief overview from the moment the cave was discovered within the Hotel des Pas to the present day, in which people continue to work on it altruistically.

Brief History of a Discovery

April 26, 1968. Tomeu Covas was building a septic tank with a pneumatic hammer — the first compressor brought to Mallorca by the Dutch owners of the Hotel Es Pas — for the hotel's wastewater, when at a few meters depth the hammer slipped through, subsequently bleeding the hole and revealing the cave.

The Cova des Pas de Vallgornera must also have or have had a natural entrance, since fossilized bat specimens have been found inside.

The First Exploration

April 17, 2008. Miquel Font Oliver, accompanied by photographer Magín Ciar, enters the cave and carries out a first exploration during which they take some photographs. Subsequently, new explorations were carried out, concluding in 1992. In July 1994, M.A. Barceló and P. Riera discover a passage to new galleries that give rise to successive explorations that have not yet been completed to this day.

What Could Have Been and Was Not

The hotel owner, with the idea of opening the cave to the public, shortly after its discovery hired two Belgian speleologists to conduct a feasibility study that gave a negative result. Thanks to this, today the Cova des Pas de Vallgornera is protected, after having been subject for a long time to acts of vandalism in part. I must also say that due to the level of protection of the cave, it cannot be visited freely and can only be done with a special permit.

Antic Sector and the New Extensions

The Cova des Pas de Vallgornera can be divided into two sectors: the Sector Antic (old sector) and the Noves Extensions (new extensions) (MERINO, 1993; 2000). In the Antic sector, topographic work was carried out from 1990 to 1992, when it was considered completed (MERINO, 1993). The development up to that point was approximately 2 km.

In 1994, Miquel Àngel Barceló and Pere Riera discover access to new galleries and chambers of the cave (Noves Extensions) and make important findings that determine that by the end of 1999 the cavity reaches a total development of 6,435 m (MERINO, 2000).

From 2002, members of the Grup Espeleòlogic Llubí and the Speleology Section of ANEM focus on reviewing specific places by investigating the existence of an air current. For two years they worked until they found a place where the air current disappeared, beginning clearing work to access a group of galleries and narrow passages between blocks that seemed to have no continuation, until Guiem Mulet, after spending several hours working among them, discovers the Sala que no Té Nom (The Room With No Name), an impressive chamber of approximately two hundred and fifty meters in length that gave way to an endless number of new galleries where work continues to this day.

Mallorca Verde Visits the Cova des Pas de Vallgornera

On June 21, 2008, I was invited by Guiem Mulet to visit the cave, which I gladly accepted as I had long wanted to see it. The group that formed that day consisted of Toni Mulet, Toni Merino, Toni Croix, Manolo Luque, Jordi Mulet, Miquel Artigues, Guiem Mulet, and myself. Later, in the Sala que no Té Nom, the group split in two, with Toni Mulet, Toni Merino, and Toni Croix continuing toward the north sector where they are exploring and surveying the area, while the other part of the group followed a different path.

As I was saying, the full group first accessed the Sala que no Té Nom, where the base camp is set up. To do this, once in the entrance room or Sala de les Arrels (Room of the Roots), we walked for a few minutes and then swam for between thirty and forty minutes through flooded galleries of indescribable beauty, notably the Llac de na Gemma (Gemma's Lake). Once the groups separated in the Sala que no Té Nom, we approached the recently discovered Sala Blanca (White Room), which very few people had visited at that point, to deliver an emergency first aid kit. To reach it, we again had to put on our neoprene suits and swim through narrow meanders, passing through the Llac Quadrat (Square Lake) until reaching the junction with the Galeria del Quilòmetre (Kilometer Gallery) from which it is accessed.

Back at the Sala que no Té Nom, we dedicated ourselves to visiting and photographing some of its beautiful corners. There are many things to see in this gigantic room, notably the substance moonmilk (photo 5), in a part of the room that I had never seen before. It is a white deposit formed by very fine crystals that, when wet, form a texture similar to cream cheese, and when dry, becomes powdery. Generally, moonmilk is made up of carbonate materials, for example, calcite, gypsum, and others. The substance moonmilk owes its name to a Swiss cave called Höhle Mondmilchloch (Moon Milk Cavern). Beautiful columns and thin, long stalactites are not lacking in the Sala que no Té Nom.

Subsequently, we went to make my first contact with the Galeria dels Espeleotemes (Gallery of Speleothems), which we reached after descending a ramp of approximately ten meters with the help of a rope installed there. Afterward, another rope installed as a handrail also facilitated walking until reaching the gallery, where we fired our cameras one last time before ending the visit to the cave after almost nine hours.

It is unforgivable to enter the cave without photographic equipment; the only problem is getting it into the cavity. I must thank Manolo Luque, who lent me a mini tripod that I was able to manage with, since the tripod that was waiting for me at the base camp could not be found.

For my part, I thank Guiem Mulet for allowing me to accompany them on a day I will never forget, and which I hope will not be the last.

Level of Protection of the Cova des Pas de Vallgornera

The Cova des Pas de Vallgornera is catalogued as a Site of Community Importance with number LIC ES5310049 of the Natura 2000 Network (Dir. 92/43 EEC).

The Cova des Pas de Vallgornera is considered a site of natural biological interest. J. A. Encinas S. Inventory of natural underground features of the Balearic Islands. 2006. JAES Editions.

Further Reading

BULLETIN No. 3 SEDECK / YEAR 2002 / SPANISH SOCIETY OF SPELEOLOGY AND KARST SCIENCES.

Official page of the Cova des Pas de Vallgornera: http://covavallgornera.org/

ANTONIO MERINO. Some unusual speleothems found in the Cova des Pas de Vallgornera. New observations. Endins, 31, 111-116. Palma de Mallorca.

ANTONI MERINO, ANTONI MULET, GUIEM MULET, ANTONI CROIX, and FRANCESC GRÀCIA. The Cova des Pas de Vallgornera (Llucmajor, Mallorca): 40 kilometers of topographic development. Endins, 31, 101-110. Palma de Mallorca.
